codeSnapshot.label: realnvp_layer.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class AffineCouplingLayer(nn.Module):
    """
    RealNVP Affine Coupling Layer (Dinh et al., 2016).
    Split x into [x1, x2]; transform x2 using functions of x1.
    x1' = x1  (pass through)
    x2' = x2 * exp(s(x1)) + t(x1)
    Inverse: x2 = (x2' - t(x1)) * exp(-s(x1))
    log|det J| = sum(s(x1))
    """
    def __init__(self, d: int, hidden: int = 64):
        super().__init__()
        d1 = d // 2
        d2 = d - d1
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d1, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, d2 * 2)  # outputs [s, t] concatenated
        )
        self.d1 = d1; self.d2 = d2

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        """Forward: x -> z, returns (z, log_det_jacobian)"""
        x1, x2 = x[:, :self.d1], x[:, self.d1:]
        st = self.net(x1)                           # [B, d2*2]
        s, t = st[:, :self.d2], st[:, self.d2:]    # scale, translation
        z2 = x2 * torch.exp(s) + t                 # affine transform
        log_det = s.sum(dim=-1)                     # sum of log-scales
        return torch.cat([x1, z2], dim=1), log_det

    def inverse(self, z: torch.Tensor):
        """Inverse: z -> x"""
        x1, z2 = z[:, :self.d1], z[:, self.d1:]
        st = self.net(x1)
        s, t = st[:, :self.d2], st[:, self.d2:]
        x2 = (z2 - t) * torch.exp(-s)
        return torch.cat([x1, x2], dim=1)

# Test
layer = AffineCouplingLayer(d=4)
x = torch.randn(8, 4)
z, ldj = layer(x)
print(f"z shape: {z.shape}, log|det J|: {ldj.shape}")  # [8,4], [8]
x_rec = layer.inverse(z)
print(f"Reconstruction error: {(x - x_rec).abs().max().item():.2e}")  # ~0

eli3: A normalising flow is a generative model that learns to transform simple random noise (like a Gaussian) into complex data (like images) through a chain of invertible transformations. Unlike VAEs (which approximate a distribution) or GANs (which can't compute exact probabilities), flows let you compute the exact probability of any data point.

Think of squeezing a lump of clay: you start with a simple shape (sphere = Gaussian) and apply a series of smooth, reversible deformations to sculpt it into a complex shape (the data distribution). Because each deformation is invertible, you can go both ways: data → noise (for density estimation) or noise → data (for generation).

The mathematical trick: the chain rule for probability densities. If z = f(x) and you know the density of z (simple Gaussian), you can compute the density of x using: log p_X(x) = log p_Z(f(x)) + log|det J_f(x)|. The Jacobian determinant adjusts for how much the transformation 'squishes or stretches' space. Flows are constrained to transformations where this Jacobian is cheap to compute.

whenWhy.title: Exact likelihood estimation for density estimation
whenWhy.body: Unlike VAEs (lower-bound on log-likelihood) and GANs (no likelihood at all), normalising flows compute exact log-probabilities: log p_θ(x) = log p_Z(f_θ(x)) + log|det J_{f_θ}(x)|. This makes flows the method of choice when exact density evaluation is required: anomaly detection (compute p(x) for test points), compression (arithmetic coding with exact probabilities), and model comparison via exact likelihood on held-out data.

whenWhy.title: Variational inference with expressive posteriors
whenWhy.body: Normalising flows can be used to improve VAE posteriors beyond the Gaussian assumption. Flow-based variational inference (Rezende & Mohamed, 2015): apply a chain of L flows to the initial Gaussian sample z_0 = μ + σ·ε: z_L = f_L ∘ ... ∘ f_1(z_0). The final z_L has a much richer, multi-modal posterior distribution while remaining tractable. The ELBO is adjusted to include the log-determinant terms from the flows. This enables the VAE to model posteriors with complex shapes (multi-modal, heavy-tailed) that the Gaussian posterior cannot represent.

whenWhy.title: Physics and scientific simulations
whenWhy.body: Normalising flows are used to sample from complex physical distributions in molecular dynamics (Boltzmann generators: normalising flows trained to sample low-energy molecular configurations) and lattice field theory (normalising flows for lattice QCD configurations). The key advantage: flows provide independent samples (unlike MCMC which has autocorrelated chains), enabling efficient estimation of thermodynamic quantities. The exact log-probability is essential for computing free energies and partition functions.

deepDiveIntro: A normalising flow is a composition of K invertible, differentiable transformations f = f_K ∘ ... ∘ f_1: x = f^{-1}(z) = f_1^{-1} ∘ ... ∘ f_K^{-1}(z), where z ~ p_Z (a simple base distribution, typically N(0,I)). By the change of variables formula: log p_X(x) = log p_Z(z) + sum_k log|det J_{f_k}(x_{k-1})|, where x_0 = x and x_k = f_k(x_{k-1}). The Jacobian det J is the key computational challenge — for a d-dimensional transformation, computing the full Jacobian det is O(d³). All flow architectures are designed to make this computation O(d) or O(d log d). [1]

The design space of flows is defined by the tradeoff between expressiveness (complex transformations → richer distribution) and tractability (cheap Jacobian computation for training and inference). NICE (Dinh et al., 2014) introduced coupling layers for O(d) Jacobian; RealNVP (Dinh et al., 2016) extended to affine coupling layers; Glow (Kingma & Dhariwal, 2018) added invertible 1×1 convolutions for channel mixing; MAF (Masked Autoregressive Flow) achieves expressive autoregressive transformations with O(d) Jacobian. The flow → normalised density direction corresponds to density estimation; the density → flow direction corresponds to generation. [2]
formula: z_K = f_K ∘ ... ∘ f_1(x),  p_X(x) = p_Z(f(x)) |det ∂f/∂x|

deepDive.heading: Change of Variables Formula
deepDive.body: This section explains the density transformation rule that makes the method exact and tractable for probabilistic modeling.

deepDive.heading: RealNVP: Affine Coupling Layers
deepDive.body: RealNVP (Real-valued Non-Volume Preserving transformations, Dinh et al., 2016) achieves tractable Jacobians via coupling layers. The functions s and t can be arbitrary (non-invertible) neural networks — they don't need to be invertible themselves because invertibility is guaranteed by the coupling layer structure. Stacking coupling layers with alternating splits (which dimensions are in x_1 vs x_2) ensures all dimensions are transformed. Glow added invertible 1×1 convolutions (parameterised as LU decomposition) to mix channels between coupling layers.

deepDive.heading: MAF and IAF: Autoregressive Flows
deepDive.body: This section explains the main idea behind this part of the method in plain language.

deepDive.heading: Continuous Normalising Flows (Neural ODEs)
deepDive.body: CNFs are more flexible than discrete flows (no coupling layer constraints) and are the basis of Flow Matching (Lipman et al., 2022) and Rectified Flows, which define straight-line ODE paths from noise to data, providing a more stable and efficient continuous flow training paradigm. Stable Diffusion 3 uses a Rectified Flow formulation.

takeaways: Change of variables: log p_X(x) = log p_Z(f(x)) + log|det J_f(x)|; exact likelihood, unlike VAE/GAN. [1]
RealNVP coupling layer: x_2' = x_2·exp(s(x_1)) + t(x_1); Jacobian = diag(exp(s)) — O(d) computation. [1]
MAF: fast density evaluation, sequential generation; IAF: parallel generation, sequential density — inverse pair. [2]
Glow adds invertible 1×1 convolutions (LU parameterisation) for flexible channel permutation between coupling layers. [2]
Continuous Normalising Flows (Neural ODEs) and Rectified Flows extend to ODE-based transformations — basis of SD3. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why are normalising flows less commonly used than VAEs and GANs for image generation?
edgeCases.a: Two main practical limitations: (1) Architectural constraint — every layer must be invertible with a tractable Jacobian determinant. This rules out standard convolutional layers (not generally invertible), pooling (not invertible), and most standard architectures. Flow layers (coupling layers, invertible 1×1 convolutions) are less expressive per parameter than standard architectures, requiring larger models for similar quality. (2) Memory — training a flow requires storing both the forward (data→noise) and inverse (noise→data) passes, which are computationally equivalent. For high-resolution images, this is memory-intensive. VAEs have an implicit regularisation via the KL term; GANs use a discriminator that is not subject to invertibility constraints. In practice, Glow can generate faces at 256×256 but requires much larger models than GANs/diffusion for comparable quality.

edgeCases.q: What is the bits-per-dimension (BPD) metric and how do flows compare to diffusion models?
edgeCases.a: Bits per dimension (BPD) = −log₂ p(x) / d, where d is the data dimensionality (d=3×H×W for images). BPD measures how many bits are needed to encode one dimension of the data — lower is better (more efficient compression = better density model). Comparison on CIFAR-10 (32×32 images, d=3072): Glow: 3.35 BPD; Flow++ (Ho et al., 2019): 3.08 BPD; Diffusion models (DDPM, Improved DDPM): ~2.94–3.27 BPD; Autoregressive (PixelCNN++): 2.92 BPD. Diffusion models and autoregressive models achieve better likelihoods than most flow-based models, while being architecturally less constrained. However, flows remain competitive for structured data (tabular, molecular) where the invertibility constraint is less limiting.

sources.label: Rezende & Mohamed (2015) — Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows, ICML 2015 arXiv:1505.05770
Dinh, Sohl-Dickstein & Bengio (2016) — Density Estimation using Real-valued Non-Volume Preserving Transformations (RealNVP), ICLR 2017 arXiv:1605.08803
